mnemonics.title: R.A.S.T.E.R.
mnemonics.content: Row-By-Row Scanning Technique - Electron beam scans row by row from top to bottom in Raster scan, like reading lines in a book

mnemonics.title: Random Means Directed
mnemonics.content: Random Scan directs the beam directly to specific points (X,Y coordinates) to draw lines/curves, unlike Raster which scans systematically

mnemonics.title: R.A.F.T.
mnemonics.content: Raster: All Frames Through - the entire frame is refreshed repeatedly at a specific refresh rate (60Hz, 72Hz)

mnemonics.title: Vector Stands for Versatile Electron Tracing
mnemonics.content: Random scan devices (also called Vector Displays) use an electron beam that traces along vector paths to create drawings

mnemonics.title: F.B.I.
mnemonics.content: Frame Buffer Image - Raster systems store pixel values in a frame buffer (bitmap) which stores complete image information

mnemonics.title: P.R.I.S.M.
mnemonics.content: Pixel Representation In Screen Matrix - In raster, screen is a matrix of pixels, each with color/intensity values stored in memory

acronyms.usage: The vacuum tube technology used in both Raster and Random scan displays - the electron gun fires electrons at a phosphorescent screen
Memory that stores the complete bitmap of pixel values for the display - used in Raster systems to refresh the screen image
Terms for the electron beam's horizontal and vertical retracing in Raster scan - beam traces horizontally, then moves down for next line
Frequency at which the screen image is redrawn per second - typically 60Hz or 72Hz in raster displays to avoid flicker
Hardware component that reads frame buffer and generates horizontal/vertical timing signals to control electron beam in raster systems

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a flashlight beam moving left-to-right across a dark wall, then quickly moving back to the left and down slightly, repeating this pattern until the entire wall is lit - this is exactly how electron beam scans the phosphorescent screen
Imagine holding a sparkler and drawing shapes in the air - the light goes exactly where you direct it, not in a fixed pattern. The beam jumps directly to coordinates where lines need to be drawn

Think of a huge grid of light switches (like in a theater sign), where each switch represents a pixel. The frame buffer stores which switches are ON/OFF and their colors - the controller reads this grid to light up the screen

Imagine a flipbook where each page is slightly different. Flipping pages quickly creates animation. The refresh rate is how many times per second the entire 'flipbook' is redrawn to create a stable image
Picture a chess board where each square can be any color. The entire screen is like this - millions of tiny squares (pixels) arranged in rows and columns, each storing a color value
Think of the electron beam like a garden hose spray - you can point it anywhere using magnetic or electric fields to direct where the water (electrons) lands on the screen

analogies.analogies: Raster is like writing text on paper - you write line by line from top to bottom, covering everything systematically. Random scan is like freehand drawing - you draw any line or shape wherever you want, not constrained to rows
Raster scan is like how old TV antennas worked - the scan line-by-line to paint the entire picture on your screen, like a painter using a single brush stroke to cover the entire wall systematically
Random scan (vector display) is like light painting photography - you move a light source in any direction to draw shapes, and the image only exists where you specifically direct the light
The frame buffer is like a detailed blueprint or stencil placed behind a perforated board - the video controller 'reads' this blueprint to know exactly where each pixel should be lit

Raster display resolution is like a mosaic art - the image is made of small colored tiles (pixels). More tiles (higher resolution) = clearer image, just like more and smaller mosaic pieces create more detailed pictures

Refresh rate is like movie frames per second - movies show 24 frames per second which our brain perceives as smooth motion. Similarly, screens refresh many times per second to create stable, flicker-free images
